schools.summary: 'All the tribes' includes the warriors of 1 Chronicles 12; the assembly follows Ishbosheth's death, and the three pleas (kinship, past leadership, divine choice) are laid out.
Gill: the tribes sent ambassadors once David had cleared himself of Abner's and Ishbosheth's deaths.
Henry: the kinship plea foreshadows the believer's plea to Christ; David's gradual, thrice-repeated anointing types the Messiah's kingdom coming by degrees.
Clarke: three good arguments — their countryman, their tried general, and God's appointed king.

debates.question: Where does the quoted promise 'Thou shalt feed my people' come from?

sides.position: a summary of God's communications to David, or an unrecorded prophet's word

schools.summary: 'Leddest out and broughtest in' is the idiom of the war-leader; the Chronicler's 'word of the LORD through Samuel' shows the secret anointing (1 Samuel 16) now fulfilled.
Wesley: 'shalt feed' means to rule and tend as a shepherd (Psalm 78:70), for the people's good and not the king's glory.
Gill: Saul had been king over all the tribes, whose submission now reverses the long division.
The elders come as the tribes' representatives; the covenant is made 'before the LORD,' as at 2 Samuel 3:21, and fulfils the word through Samuel.
Wesley: a mutual covenant — David to rule by God's laws, the people to obey; Clarke marks this as the third anointing, over the whole kingdom.
Gill: the assembled elders are the very ones the tribes had sent, now meeting the king in a body.

debates.question: What was the 'league' David made with Israel?

sides.position: not a constitutional monarchy, but a covenant on specific matters (defence, sparing Saul's house, no tribal partiality)
a mutual bond — the king to rule by God's law, the people to obey

schools.summary: Age thirty, reign forty; the figures (given at the close in Chronicles) fix ~10-12 years since Goliath and make David seventy at his death.
Gill: thirty was the age of David's antitype Christ at the outset of his ministry (Luke 3:23).
Henry: thirty is the age the Levites began service (Numbers 4:3) and the Son of David his ministry — the season of mature strength.
The six-month fraction (2 Samuel 2:11; 1 Chronicles 3:4) is dropped from round summaries; sacred historians omit or round fractions, producing the indefiniteness seen in the kings' chronologies.
Gill: seven and a half years measure how long the crown lingered in Saul's house; David was near thirty-eight when made king of all.
Henry: Hebron was famous and a priest's city, but Jerusalem was to be the holy city, the city of David, named to the Bible's end (Revelation 21).
The Jebusites trust Zion's natural strength; Ellicott renders 'the blind and the lame shall keep thee off' and traces the city's history from Joshua to the Jebusite reoccupation.
Wesley and Clarke: the Jebusites set invalids on the walls in contempt of David, so impregnable did they think their fort.
Henry: their confidence lay in fortifications or in idol-gods David scorns as 'blind and lame'; the enemies of God are most secure when their fall is near.
Gill: part of Jerusalem lay in Benjamin, so David waited till all Israel had made him king.

debates.question: Who are 'the blind and the lame' of the Jebusite taunt?

sides.position: literal invalids set on the walls to mock David
possibly the Jebusites' idol-gods, contemptuously so named by David

schools.summary: Zion (from 'dry/parched') was the loftiest, last-held height; 'the same is the city of David' is written by anticipation of v.9, the lower town having fallen after Joshua's death (Judges 1:8).
Gill: the strong hold of Zion stood apart and very strong; taking it opened the city, though the taking itself was hard.

'Gutter' (tsinnor, only here and Psalm 42:7) is a water-shaft; a vowel change makes 'getteth' into 'hurl,' so David flings the enemy's taunt back; the reward-clause and Joab's feat come from 1 Chronicles 11:6.
Wesley: whoever scales the fort to the gutter; 'hated of David's soul' names the insolent Jebusites; the saying became a proverb kept to remember the exploit.
Gill: 'on that day' is the day Zion fell.

outliers.note: Notes that Joab, answering the challenge, went up first and won command of Israel's armies, which later bound David to him.

schools.summary: David makes the citadel his palace and Jerusalem his capital for its strength and central position; 'the Millo' is an old Canaanite fortification, strengthened later by Solomon and Hezekiah.
Henry: the once-threatening fort becomes David's safety; the church is called Zion, and the heart's idols must be dispossessed before the Son of David reigns there.
Gill: David dwelt in the stronghold of Zion he had taken.

themes: Zion as type of the heart

schools.summary: David 'went on and grew great' in power and fame — but the clause governing all is 'for the LORD God of hosts was with him.'
Gill: he grew great in honour, wealth, and dominion, subduing his enemies.
Henry: those who have the Lord of hosts for them need not fear any host; greatness must be ascribed to God's presence — which Saul forfeited.

themes: God's presence as the secret of the reign

schools.summary: Hiram of Tyre, ally of David and later of Solomon, sends cedar and builders; Ellicott's chronology places the embassy some years after Jerusalem's fall, in the interval of David's growing greatness.
Henry: built by the sons of the stranger, David's house is no less fit to be dedicated to God — a figure of the gospel church (Isaiah 60:10).
Clarke: Hiram was a friendly prince and, no doubt, a believer in the true God.
Gill: this Hiram is the father of the Hiram who served Solomon, his earlier name Abibalus.

debates.question: Is this Hiram the same king who later served Solomon?

sides.position: the same man across both reigns (the embassy falling late in David's reign)

schools.summary: David 'perceived' from his success that Jehovah had established and exalted his kingdom, and (Ellicott) that his blessings came for the sake of God's chosen people, not himself.
Henry: David's kingdom is established (unlike Saul's) because he types Christ (Psalm 89); and he perceives God's favour, which many have but do not discern.
Wesley: exalted 'for Israel's sake,' that he might be a blessing and they be happy under him.
Gill: he perceived it by the prosperity attending all he set his hand to.

themes: established as a type of Christ

schools.summary: Taking many wives broke the king's law (Deuteronomy 17:17); K&D and Ellicott judge David seduced by oriental custom, coming perilously near Solomon's excess and foreshadowing the Bathsheba fall.
Clarke: polygamy is against nature and God's first design; Wesley reckons it among David's miscarriages, a fence broken through which worse would come.
Gill: not to his honour, and contrary to the law of God.
Henry: 'we praise him not, we justify him not' — the many wives did not keep him from coveting Bathsheba.
The list, with spelling variants, matches 1 Chronicles 3; the first four are Bathsheba's sons; Solomon, though listed last, was her eldest (2 Samuel 12:24).
Clarke: the Hebrew names eleven, the Septuagint twenty-four, with evident textual corruption.
Gill: these are the sons, the daughters unnamed, born of David's wives.
Gill: the Elishua named here is called Elishama in the parallel list — a variation of spelling, not of person.
The Chronicles rolls (1 Chronicles 3:6; 14:5) vary chiefly in spelling, the lists correcting one another.
Set beside 1 Chronicles 3:6-8, the shorter list here is an abridgement: two names (a second Eliphelet and Nogah) are omitted, most likely as dying young or without issue.
Gill, following Kimchi: the two Chronicler's names are dropped because they may have died without sons.

themes: the calm before the Philistine assault

schools.summary: 'To seek David' means to hunt him down; since he went down then up, 'the hold' is disputed — K&D take the wilderness stronghold of the Saul years.
Gill: the Philistines struck now that Israel was united, strong, and master of Jerusalem; Kimchi assigns Psalm 2 to this hour.
Clarke and Wesley: they attacked before David's army could grow; 'the hold' a fortified post toward the enemy where his people could rally.
Henry: David was raised up to save Israel from the Philistines; as the Messiah's kingdom was assailed at its rise, 'the heathen raged' in vain.

debates.question: What is 'the hold' to which David went down?

sides.position: the wilderness stronghold in the desert of Judah from the Saul years
some stronghold near the Philistine army, not Zion nor Adullam
a fortified place toward the Philistines where his people could rally

schools.summary: Rephaim, the 'valley of the giants' (Joshua 15:8), a fruitful valley SW of Jerusalem; that the foe came here 'to seek David' proves Jerusalem had already fallen to him.
Henry: they aimed at Jerusalem before it could be fortified; their spreading shows their numbers, but the wider they spread, the fairer mark for God's arrows (Revelation 20:9).
Gill: the valley of the giants, west of Jerusalem; their spreading shows they were very numerous.

themes: the wide array a fair mark

schools.summary: David inquired by Abiathar and the Urim; the twofold inquiry here and at v.23 disproves the claim that the sacred lot fell out of use after Saul.
Henry: a twofold question — conscience asks the duty ('Shall I go up?'), prudence the success ('Wilt thou deliver them?'); David trusts the promise above his army.
Clarke: David counted himself only the captain of the LORD's host and would not strike a stroke without command.
Baal-perazim = 'lord of breaches'; David's onset burst like pent waters, a victory remembered as a byword for divine aid (Isaiah 28:21); the name has no reference to the god Baal.
Clarke and Wesley: David 'ascribes all to God,' the name commemorating the LORD's own breaking-forth like floods that overturn all before them.
Henry: David gives God the glory — 'I could not have done it if he had not done it before me' — and names the place that posterity might mark God's honour (Psalm 115:1).
Gill: the place was named by anticipation, about three miles from Jerusalem toward Bethlehem.

themes: a monument of praise, not trophy

schools.summary: The Philistines brought idols to war as Israel once brought the ark; leaving them shows the rout's completeness; 'burned' comes from 1 Chronicles 14:12, David disposing of them by the law (Deuteronomy 7:5).
Henry: fleeing, they could not carry their gods, a burden to weary beasts (Isaiah 46:1); God makes men cast their idols to the moles and bats (Isaiah 2:20).
Wesley's irony: when the ark fell to the Philistines it consumed them, but these images could not save themselves from being consumed.
Gill: the custom of many nations to carry their gods to battle, in imitation of Israel's ark.
Though beaten, the Philistines return to the same valley to repair their loss, David not having followed up the first victory.
Henry: they came up again to recover their losses, 'their hearts hardened to their destruction.'
Wesley: 'spread themselves' shows their numbers (Revelation 20:9), but the wider they spread, the fairer mark for God's arrows.
Gill: they returned, by Josephus with an army three times larger than before.
David inquires again; the LORD changes the order — not a frontal assault but a flanking march to the baca-shrubs; the rare word (only here and 1 Chronicles 14:14) is uncertain.
Clarke: God will not work without means, teaching caution and dependence together; Wesley: 'God's purposes and promises do not exclude men's endeavours.'
Henry: David is bidden draw back and stand still to see the salvation of the LORD (Exodus 14:13).
Gill: though he had prevailed before, David would not engage again without God's will.

themes: God does not repeat his method

schools.summary: 'The sound of a going' is the march of an army of God (Genesis 32; 2 Kings 6:17; Judges 5:4; Psalm 68:7); 'bestir thyself' means, literally, be sharp — act quickly.
Henry: God charges the enemy by an invisible host of angels, yet David must bestir himself and pursue — grace quickens our endeavours (Philippians 2:12); the sound types Pentecost (Acts 2:2).
Clarke and Wesley: probably a rustling among the leaves that God times as a sign, lest David credit his own stratagem, and to affright the enemy.
Gill relays Jarchi and R. Isaiah: the sound signified angels sent to help David.

debates.question: What is 'the sound of a going in the tops of the mulberry trees'?

sides.position: a supernatural march of angels sent to fight for David
a natural rustling of wind, divinely timed as a signal

themes: God goes before, yet man must move

schools.summary: 'Geba' is a scribal slip for 'Gibeon' (1 Chronicles 14:16), which lies on the way to Gezer; the north-westerly chase follows from David's flanking march.
Clarke: David punctually obeyed; such divine guidance is not given now because it is neither asked nor expected for want of faith; David the shepherd feeds, not oppresses, the people.
Henry: David waited till God moved, then struck — as the apostles awaited the Spirit before going forth 'conquering, and to conquer' (Revelation 6:2).
Gill: David did all as the LORD commanded, where Saul did not.

debates.question: Is the pursuit 'from Geba' or 'from Gibeon'?

sides.position: 'Gibeon' is the true reading; 'Geba' a scribal slip
